About

I hold two PhDs in Thermofluids and Mechanical Engineering and currently serve as a Research Assistant in the Department of Mechanical Engineering at Université de Sherbrooke (UDS), under the supervision of Prof. Sébastien Poncet and Prof. Leyla Amiri. My 2nd PhD focuses on high-temperature sensible thermal energy storage — specifically magnetite rock bed systems — validated experimentally and through CFD simulation.

My research focuses on the implementation of thermal and cold energy storage (TES/CTES) systems within renewable energy architectures — a critical pathway for decarbonizing the global energy mix. I work extensively with CFD Simulation, the Lattice Boltzmann (LBM), phase change materials (PCM), nanofluids, and heat exchanger optimization.

I am the author or co-author of over 120 publications, including 95 papers in peer-reviewed international journals, with more than 4,700 citations. I have been recognized on Scopus/Ioannidis' World's Top 2% Scientists list annually since 2022, and ranked #23 nationally in Engineering & Technology by Research.com's 2025 Rising Stars in Canada ranking.

Beyond research, I have over a decade of university lecturing experience, teaching HVAC, Advanced Convection Heat Transfer, and Advanced Heat Exchangers at institutions in Iran and Canada.

Research

Areas of Expertise

Phase Change Material (PCM) storage systems represent one of the most promising technologies in latent thermal energy storage. PCMs store and release large amounts of thermal energy during their phase transition — typically from solid to liquid — at a nearly constant temperature, making them significantly more energy-dense than sensible storage media. This isothermality is particularly valuable in applications requiring precise temperature control, such as building thermal management, cold chain logistics, solar energy storage, and industrial waste heat recovery.

How PCM storage systems work: During the charging phase, a heat transfer fluid (HTF) delivers thermal energy to the PCM, causing it to melt and absorb latent heat. During the discharging phase, the PCM solidifies, releasing the stored energy back to the HTF on demand. The key challenge in PCM systems is the inherently low thermal conductivity of most organic and inorganic PCMs, which limits the speed of heat transfer during both charging and discharging — a problem my research directly addresses.

My research on PCM systems focuses on numerical investigation of melting and solidification processes in various storage geometries using computational fluid dynamics (CFD) and the enthalpy-porosity method. I examine how storage geometry, fin configuration, nanoparticle enhancement, and operating conditions influence the thermal performance and melt fraction evolution over time.

  • Melting and solidification dynamics in cylindrical, annular, and shell-and-tube PCM storage geometries
  • Effect of storage geometry (aspect ratio, diameter, orientation) on melt fraction and thermal performance
  • Fin-enhanced PCM systems: longitudinal, radial, and tree-shaped fins to accelerate charge/discharge rates
  • Nano-enhanced PCM (nePCM): dispersing nanoparticles (Al₂O₃, TiO₂, CuO) to improve thermal conductivity
  • Enthalpy-porosity method for accurate numerical modeling of solid–liquid phase transitions
  • Natural convection effects during PCM melting and their role in heat transfer enhancement
  • Cold Thermal Energy Storage (CTES) using ice slurry and encapsulated PCM systems
  • Triplex-tube and double-pipe heat exchanger configurations for PCM storage integration

Cold Thermal Energy Storage (CTES) using ice is one of the most effective and widely deployed latent heat storage technologies for building cooling applications. By producing ice during off-peak hours — typically overnight when electricity tariffs are low and grid demand is minimal — and melting it during peak cooling demand hours, ice storage systems significantly reduce peak electrical load, lower operating costs, and enable more efficient use of chiller capacity.

Ice storage was the central focus of my first PhD (2014–2020) at Babol Noshirvani University of Technology (BNUT), where I conducted extensive numerical and experimental investigations of Cold Thermal Energy Storage Systems (CTESSs). This work gave me deep expertise in the thermodynamics of ice formation and melting, the design of ice-on-coil and ice slurry systems, and the integration of CTES with building HVAC and district cooling networks.

Types of ice storage systems:

  • Ice-on-coil (external melt): Ice forms on the outside of refrigerant or brine coils submerged in a tank; warm return water flows over the ice surface to melt it during discharge
  • Ice-on-coil (internal melt): Refrigerant or warm brine flows through the coil interior to melt the ice from within, providing more controlled discharge rates
  • Encapsulated ice (ice balls/modules): Water is encapsulated in small spherical or modular containers packed in a tank; brine flows around them to freeze or melt the contents
  • Ice slurry: A pumpable mixture of small ice crystals suspended in a carrier fluid; highly flexible and offers very high heat transfer rates due to large surface area
  • Ice harvester: Ice sheets form on flat evaporator plates and are periodically harvested into a storage tank, allowing continuous ice production

My research on ice storage covers the numerical simulation of freezing and melting processes in various CTES geometries, the influence of fin arrays and flow configuration on charge/discharge rates, the role of natural convection during ice melting, and the system-level performance of CTES integrated with centrifugal and screw chillers in commercial building applications.

  • Freezing and melting dynamics in cylindrical and annular ice storage units
  • Fin-enhanced ice-on-coil systems for accelerated freezing and melting
  • Natural convection effects during ice melting and their enhancement
  • Ice slurry generation, transport, and heat transfer characteristics
  • Encapsulated ice module systems: void fraction and thermal performance
  • System integration: CTES with chillers, cooling towers, and AHUs
  • Load-shifting strategies and economic analysis of ice storage in buildings
  • Cold chain and district cooling applications of large-scale ice TES

Sensible Thermal Energy Storage (Sensible TES) stores thermal energy by raising the temperature of a solid or liquid medium without any phase change. Among sensible TES technologies, Rock Bed Thermal Energy Storage (RBTES) is a particularly promising and cost-effective solution for medium-to-high temperature applications — and is the system I have focused on in my current research.

A rock bed TES system consists of a packed bed of crushed rock or gravel through which a heat transfer fluid (typically air or gas) flows to charge or discharge the storage. Due to the high availability, low cost, and non-toxicity of rock as a storage medium, RBTES is especially well-suited for industrial waste heat recovery, solar thermal integration, and off-grid energy resilience.

My numerical research investigates the coupled fluid-solid heat transfer dynamics within the packed bed during charging and discharging cycles, with attention to thermal stratification, void fraction effects, pressure drop, and long-term cyclic efficiency.

  • CFD modeling of airflow and heat transfer in packed rock beds
  • Thermal stratification and thermocline development during charge/discharge
  • Effect of rock size, shape, and void fraction on thermal performance
  • Pressure drop and pumping power analysis in packed bed configurations
  • Charging and discharging efficiency under cyclic operating conditions
  • Integration of RBTES with solar thermal and industrial waste heat systems
  • Comparison of rock bed performance vs. other sensible TES media (molten salt, concrete)
Decarbonizing the Future: Medium to High Temperature Thermal and Mechanical Storages for Off-Grid Resilience

The Volume-Average (or Volume-Averaged) Model — also known as the macroscopic or continuum approach — treats the porous medium as a continuous, homogeneous domain by averaging the governing equations of fluid flow and heat transfer over a representative elementary volume (REV). Rather than resolving the complex pore geometry explicitly, this approach uses effective transport properties — such as effective thermal conductivity, permeability, and porosity — to represent the averaged behavior of the porous structure at the macroscale.

Within this framework, two thermal sub-models are commonly employed. The Local Thermal Equilibrium (LTE) model assumes that the fluid and solid phases within each REV are at the same temperature — a valid assumption for systems with high fluid–solid contact area and slow flow rates. The more rigorous Local Thermal Non-Equilibrium (LTNE) model relaxes this assumption and solves two separate energy equations — one for the fluid phase and one for the solid phase — coupled through an interstitial heat transfer coefficient. The LTNE model is essential for accurately capturing the physics in high heat flux systems, transient thermal storage (such as rock bed TES), and metal foam heat exchangers where significant temperature differences exist between fluid and solid.

The momentum equation in volume-averaged models is described by the Darcy–Brinkman–Forchheimer extended model, which accounts for viscous drag (Brinkman term) and inertial effects at higher flow velocities (Forchheimer term) in addition to the classical Darcy pressure-drop term.

  • Representative Elementary Volume (REV) averaging methodology
  • Local Thermal Equilibrium (LTE) model: single energy equation, valid for slow flows
  • Local Thermal Non-Equilibrium (LTNE) model: dual energy equations for fluid and solid phases
  • Darcy–Brinkman–Forchheimer extended momentum equation
  • Effective thermal conductivity and permeability as functions of porosity
  • Interstitial (fluid–solid) heat transfer coefficient determination
  • Application to rock bed TES, metal foam channels, and packed bed reactors
  • Validation of LTNE model against experimental rock bed data
Cyclic performance of high-temperature magnetite rock bed thermal energy storage: An experimental study
Mousavi Ajarostaghi, S. S., Poncet, S., & Amiri, L.  ·  Next Energy, 12, 100616  ·  2026
Experimental verification of a local thermal non-equilibrium model for a horizontal cylindrical magnetite-based rock bed as a thermal battery
Mousavi Ajarostaghi, S. S., Amiri, L., & Poncet, S.  ·  CSME-CFDSC-CSR 2025 International Congress · Progress in Canadian Mechanical Engineering, vol. 8 · 2025
Thermal mixing and flow characteristics in a T-shaped micromixer with porous media inserts using the Lattice Boltzmann Method
International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer · 2015

The Pore-Scale Model resolves the actual geometry of individual pores and solid grains, simulating fluid flow and heat transfer directly within the pore space without any averaging assumptions. Rather than relying on effective properties, this approach captures the local physics at the microscale — including the precise velocity field within each pore, local temperature gradients at the fluid–solid interface, and the influence of pore geometry, connectivity, and tortuosity on transport phenomena.

Pore-scale simulations are computationally intensive but provide invaluable insight into the microscopic mechanisms that drive macroscale behavior — such as how pore morphology affects permeability, where hot spots develop at the solid surface during convective heating, and how fluid–solid heat transfer rates depend on the local flow structure. These microscale insights can then be used to derive or validate the effective property correlations used in volume-averaged models, bridging the gap between pore-scale physics and continuum-level design.

My pore-scale research uses the Lattice Boltzmann (LBM) — a mesoscale numerical approach particularly well-suited to complex pore geometries — to simulate flow and heat transfer in reconstructed porous structures. I apply this approach to T-shaped micromixers with porous inserts, packed beds, and idealized pore geometries to study local heat transfer coefficients, mixing efficiency, and fluid–solid thermal interaction at the pore level.

  • Lattice Boltzmann Method (LBM) for pore-scale flow and heat transfer
  • D2Q9 and D3Q19 lattice schemes for 2D and 3D pore-scale simulations
  • Pore geometry reconstruction from idealized and realistic packed structures
  • Local Nusselt number and heat transfer coefficient at fluid–solid interfaces
  • Tortuosity and effective diffusivity in complex pore networks
  • Thermal mixing at the pore scale in T-shaped micromixers with porous inserts
  • Upscaling: bridging pore-scale results to volume-averaged effective properties
  • Comparison of pore-scale LBM results with LTNE volume-average predictions
Thermal mixing, cooling and entropy generation in a micromixer with a porous zone by the lattice Boltzmann method

My research on Heat Transfer Enhancement targets the enhancement of convective heat transfer by suspending engineered nanoparticles (Al₂O₃, TiO₂, CuO, hybrid combinations) in base fluids and studying their thermo-physical behavior in advanced heat exchanger geometries.

I combine nanofluid flow with geometrical modifications — helical double-pipe configurations, curved conical turbulators, and perforated inserts — to achieve significant improvements in the Nusselt number and thermal performance factor.

  • Hybrid nanofluids (Al₂O₃/TiO₂, Cu/Al₂O₃) in helical heat exchangers
  • Curved conical turbulator design and optimization
  • Nusselt number and friction factor correlation development
  • Thermal performance factor (TPF) assessment
  • Double-pipe and shell-and-tube heat exchanger enhancement
  • Nature-inspired surface textures for passive heat transfer augmentation

My work in Solar Energy centres on improving the thermal efficiency of solar collectors and integrating them with thermal storage to enable continuous energy delivery independent of solar irradiance cycles.

I conduct comprehensive numerical analyses and reviews of evacuated tube solar collectors (ETSCs) and parabolic trough collectors (PTCs), examining how nanofluid working fluids, turbulator inserts, and helical fin geometries enhance heat transfer and reduce thermal losses. This work contributes directly to solar-driven HVAC systems and solar-TES hybrid architectures for off-grid and decarbonized energy applications.

  • Evacuated tube solar collectors (ETSC) with nanofluid enhancement
  • Parabolic trough collector (PTC) performance optimization
  • Hybrid nanofluid flow in solar receiver tubes with helical fins
  • Heat loss analysis and optical efficiency improvement
  • Solar-thermal integration with cold and thermal energy storage
  • Solar-driven HVAC applications for sustainable buildings

Renewable Energy Integration with Thermal Storage addresses one of the central challenges in the energy transition: the intermittency of renewable sources such as solar and wind. My research investigates how thermal energy storage systems — sensible (rock bed), latent (PCM/ice), and hybrid — can be coupled with renewable energy sources to provide continuous, dispatchable heat and power, even when the primary energy source is unavailable.

A key focus is the Power-to-Heat (P2H) concept, in which surplus renewable electricity — generated during off-peak periods when demand is low — is converted to thermal energy and stored for later use. This approach smooths grid fluctuations, reduces curtailment of renewables, and enables decarbonization of industrial heat supply and remote off-grid communities. My CSME 2024 bulletin article directly addresses this topic, examining medium-to-high temperature mechanical and thermal storage solutions for off-grid resilience.

  • Power-to-Heat (P2H): converting surplus renewable electricity to stored thermal energy
  • Coupling rock bed TES and PCM systems with solar and wind energy sources
  • Off-grid resilience: TES-based energy autonomy for remote communities
  • Decarbonization of industrial heat supply through renewable-TES hybrid systems
  • Charging/discharging strategy optimization under intermittent renewable supply
  • Techno-economic comparison of sensible, latent, and hybrid TES for renewable integration
  • System-level efficiency of solar-TES and wind-TES coupled architectures
Decarbonizing the Future: Medium to High Temperature Thermal and Mechanical Storages for Off-Grid Resilience
CSME Spring Bulletin · 2024

Geothermal Energy harnesses the thermal energy stored within the earth — from shallow ground heat accessible a few metres below the surface, to deep hydrothermal reservoirs at hundreds or thousands of metres depth. As a renewable, weather-independent, and highly reliable energy source, geothermal energy is increasingly recognised as a cornerstone technology for sustainable heating, cooling, and power generation.

My research focuses on Ground Heat Exchangers (GHEs) — engineered systems that transfer heat between the ground and a building's HVAC system via a circulating heat carrier fluid. I investigate both horizontal and vertical GHE configurations, examining their thermal performance, long-term ground temperature recovery, and integration with ground-source heat pump (GSHP) systems.

Geothermal (Borehole) Heat Exchanger

Horizontal Ground Heat Exchangers (HGHEs) are shallow geothermal systems installed at depths typically ranging from 0.8 to 2.5 metres below the surface. Because they lie within the zone of seasonal temperature fluctuation, they are more influenced by ambient air temperature, precipitation, and solar radiation compared to vertical systems. However, they require significantly lower drilling costs and are well suited for sites with sufficient land area.

The most common horizontal configurations include single-pipe, double-pipe, slinky (coiled), and spiral arrangements. Slinky coils, in which pipes are wound in overlapping loops, achieve higher heat exchange rates per unit trench length, making them a popular choice where land area is limited. Spiral or basket-type collectors are compact vertical-oriented loops placed in horizontal trenches, offering an intermediate solution between conventional horizontal and vertical systems.

My research on horizontal GHEs investigates the thermal performance and soil temperature recovery of these systems under various climatic and soil conditions, with a focus on optimizing pipe spacing, burial depth, and coil geometry to maximize heat extraction rates while ensuring long-term thermal sustainability of the ground.

  • Single-pipe, double-pipe, and parallel horizontal GHE configurations
  • Slinky (coiled) and spiral basket GHE heat transfer optimization
  • Effect of burial depth and pipe spacing on thermal performance
  • Soil temperature recovery between heating seasons
  • Influence of solar radiation, rainfall, and seasonal frost on shallow GHEs
  • CFD modeling of soil–pipe coupled heat and moisture transfer
  • Comparative performance of horizontal vs. vertical GHE configurations
Numerical analysis of thermal performance of slinky-type horizontal geothermal heat exchanger equipped with turbulator
Progress in Canadian Mechanical Engineering  ·  2023
Hybrid nanofluid flow in a horizontal borehole heat exchanger with twisted tape as turbulator
Proc. 11th Int. Conf. on Innovation and Research in Engineering Sciences  ·  2022

Vertical Borehole Heat Exchangers (BHEs) are the most widely deployed ground-source heat exchange technology for building heating and cooling. They consist of pipes inserted into vertical boreholes — typically 50 to 300 metres deep — drilled into the earth and grouted to ensure good thermal contact with the surrounding rock or soil. At these depths, the ground temperature remains virtually constant year-round (equal to the mean annual surface temperature of the region), providing a reliable and stable thermal source/sink independent of seasonal weather variations.

The principal configurations include single U-tube, double U-tube, and coaxial (pipe-in-pipe) BHEs. A heat carrier fluid — typically water or an antifreeze solution — circulates through the pipes, absorbing heat from the ground in winter (heating mode) or rejecting heat to the ground in summer (cooling mode). Grout — a thermally enhanced cement or bentonite mix — fills the annular space between the pipes and the borehole wall to maximise conductive heat transfer and prevent groundwater contamination.

My research focuses on numerical and analytical modeling of heat transfer within and around vertical BHEs, covering borehole thermal resistance, grout conductivity effects, soil stratification, and the long-term thermal behaviour of borehole fields. I also investigate Borehole Thermal Energy Storage (BTES) systems, in which large arrays of boreholes are used for seasonal storage of solar or waste heat.

  • Single U-tube, double U-tube, and coaxial BHE configurations
  • Borehole thermal resistance and grout conductivity optimization
  • Soil and rock stratification effects on long-term BHE performance
  • Thermal interference between boreholes in large borehole fields
  • Borehole Thermal Energy Storage (BTES) for seasonal heat storage
  • Thermal response tests (TRT) and in-situ parameter estimation
  • Ground-source heat pump (GSHP) integration and COP optimization
  • Hybrid systems: BHE coupled with solar thermal collectors
  • CFD simulation of convective heat transfer inside BHE pipes and annulus

CFD Simulation is the primary numerical tool in my research. Using ANSYS Fluent and finite volume methods, I simulate complex coupled heat and fluid flow problems to understand and optimize thermal systems before physical prototyping.

My CFD work spans from micro-scale mixing in T-shaped micromixers to macro-scale heat exchanger and solar collector systems. I employ turbulence modeling, conjugate heat transfer, and multiphase solvers depending on the application.

  • Turbulence modeling (k-ε, k-ω SST) in heat exchanger geometries
  • Turbulator inserts: conical rings, wire coils, twisted tapes, helical fins
  • Conjugate heat transfer in parabolic trough solar collectors
  • Swirl generators and vortex-induced enhancement techniques
  • Nature-inspired surface modifications (shark skin, biomimetic)
  • Mesh independence studies and validation with experimental data

Simulation Results

🖼Add result image
🖼Add result image
🖼Add result image
🖼Add result image

The Lattice Boltzmann (LBM) is a mesoscale computational approach I use to simulate fluid dynamics and heat transfer phenomena at scales where continuum assumptions may break down — particularly in porous media, microchannels, and phase-transition problems.

Unlike conventional CFD solvers, LBM operates by tracking the statistical distribution of fluid particles on a discrete lattice, enabling natural handling of complex geometries, multiphase flows, and interfacial dynamics.

  • D2Q9 and D3Q19 lattice schemes for 2D/3D flow simulation
  • LBM applied to porous media flow and heat transfer
  • Multiphase LBM for liquid-vapor phase change
  • Thermal LBM for natural and forced convection
  • Micro-scale flow simulation in T-shaped micromixers
  • Comparative studies: LBM vs. conventional Navier-Stokes solvers
Thermal mixing, cooling and entropy generation in a micromixer with a porous zone by the lattice Boltzmann method
Journal of Thermal Analysis and Calorimetry  ·  2020
Thermal mixing in T-shaped micromixers with a porous block by the lattice Boltzmann method: influence of the mixing channel configuration
